summary: USTR recommends tighter USMCA auto rules of origin. Sheinbaum takes cautious stance on possible new US tariffs against 60 countries. Forced-labor complaint over Chinese camera imports tests USMCA mechanism.

lead.h1: USTR recommends tighter USMCA auto rules of origin
lead.context: The trade office cites a shrinking US content share in vehicles assembled in Mexico and Canada and regional reliance on chips and circuit boards sourced outside North America.
lead.background: USMCA rules of origin set the regional content vehicles need to qualify for tariff-free treatment, and the pact now undergoes annual reviews after Washington declined a 16-year extension.
lead.drivers: USTR points to a shrinking US content share in vehicles built in Mexico and Canada and to regional reliance on chips, circuit boards and display panels from non-market economies.
lead.implications: Mexico faces 25% duties on vehicles and parts that fail to meet the qualifying content threshold.

coverage.title: USTR recommends tighter USMCA auto rules of origin
coverage.summary: The trade office cites a shrinking US content share in vehicles assembled in Mexico and Canada and regional reliance on chips and circuit boards sourced outside North America.

items.headline: Sheinbaum takes cautious stance on possible new US tariffs against 60 countries
items.context: The economy ministry says it presented arguments to US officials for why Mexico should be excluded from any new round of tariffs.
items.background: USMCA's first joint review began after the US declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, and Washington also enforces Section 301 tariffs against countries linked to forced-labor production or unfair trade practices.
items.drivers: Greer told CNBC the new tariffs would target roughly 60 countries where goods are found to involve forced labor or unfair commercial practices.
items.next: Sheinbaum said Mexico must wait for an official US government announcement on which countries the new Section 301 tariffs will cover.

coverage.title: Sheinbaum takes cautious stance on possible new US tariffs against 60 countries
coverage.summary: The economy ministry says it presented arguments to US officials for why Mexico should be excluded from any new round of tariffs.

items.headline: Forced-labor complaint over Chinese camera imports tests USMCA mechanism
items.context: Civil groups file what they call the first citizen-driven case under the trade pact, accusing Mexico of allowing imports linked to forced labor, a claim that could trigger formal review procedures with the US.
items.background: Article 23.6 of USMCA lets citizens file complaints over forced-labor imports, and the US has proposed a 10% minimum tariff for most partners on goods linked to forced labor.
items.drivers: Civil groups ProDESC and Empower revived a complaint over Dahua and Hikvision surveillance cameras tied to Xinjiang supply chains, filed with the labor ministry in 2023 and dismissed that same year.
items.implications: The case adds to US scrutiny of Mexico during the T-MEC review at the same time Mexico is trying to avoid the proposed 10% forced-labor tariff.

coverage.title: Forced-labor complaint over Chinese camera imports tests USMCA mechanism
coverage.summary: Civil groups file what they call the first citizen-driven case under the trade pact, accusing Mexico of allowing imports linked to forced labor, a claim that could trigger formal review procedures with the US.

items.headline: Mexico's economy seen growing 1.7% in June, led by services
items.context: Services drove the gain while investment remained the main drag, according to early estimates ahead of INEGI's formal second-quarter data.
items.background: INEGI's IOAE is an early, unofficial estimate of economic activity published ahead of the formal quarterly GDP report, used to gauge momentum before official data lands.
items.drivers: May's estimated growth of 1.6% and April's 2.2% feed into a projected 1.8% for the second quarter, well above the first quarter's 0.3%.
items.implications: The acceleration would mark the strongest quarterly pace in about two years and lift first-half growth to 1.1% from 0.4% a year earlier.

coverage.title: Mexico's economy seen growing 1.7% in June, led by services
coverage.summary: Services drove the gain while investment remained the main drag, according to early estimates ahead of INEGI's formal second-quarter data.

items.headline: Moody's warns on falling federal transfers to states as welfare spending rises
items.context: Federal revenue shared with states grew just 0.9% annually between January and May, even as scholarship and pension programs squeeze the finance ministry's budget.
items.background: The Recaudación Federal Participable (RFP) is the pool of federal tax revenue Mexico shares with states and municipalities, and welfare programs like scholarships and pensions draw on the same federal budget.
items.drivers: Moody's Local points to slowing national growth and the resulting slowdown in RFP collection, while spending on social transfers keeps rising and squeezing Hacienda's operating budget.
items.implications: Moody's says the mismatch between slowing revenue and rising operating spending could shrink operating margins for states and municipalities by the end of 2026.

coverage.title: Moody's warns on falling federal transfers to states as welfare spending rises
coverage.summary: Federal revenue shared with states grew just 0.9% annually between January and May, even as scholarship and pension programs squeeze the finance ministry's budget.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.40 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.
